did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a fusion of handcrafted features and features extracted from pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including AlexNet, VggNet, GoogLeNet, and ResNet to classify melanoma skin cancer. It also employs a Genetic Algorithm for feature selection/engineering.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel skin-lesion classification approach based on the fusion of handcrafted features and pre-trained CNN features (AlexNet, VggNet, GoogLeNet, ResNet) with Genetic Algorithm-based feature selection. It presents original experimental results, including a comparative study on PH2 and ISIC datasets, confusion matrices, and performance metrics (Accuracy, F-measure, Kappa), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Efficient fusion of handcrafted and pre-trained CNNs features to classify melanoma skin cancer
brief_summary: This study proposes a skin-lesion classification approach for melanoma detection by fusing handcrafted features (shape, skeleton, color, texture) with features extracted from pre-trained deep learning architectures, specifically AlexNet, VggNet, GoogLeNet, and ResNet. To address the limitations of machine learning and deep learning on varying dataset sizes, the authors employ a Genetic Algorithm for feature selection to remove redundancy and identify relevant features from a pool of 4034 descriptors. The proposed method is validated and tested on both small and large datasets, including the PH2 and ISIC databases. A comparative study is conducted against other recent approaches to evaluate performance. The results demonstrate that the feature-fusion-based approach is promising and effectively combines the strengths of both machine learning and deep learning methods for melanoma classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for classification in the proposed skin-lesion classification approach. The study aims to distinguish melanoma from non-melanoma lesions.

The comparator class used in the binary classification task (melanoma vs. non-melanoma). The paper mentions distinguishing between melanoma and non-melanoma, and between melanoma and benign lesions. Specific benign subtypes (e.g., nevus, seborrheic keratosis) are mentioned in references but not explicitly detailed as separate experimental classes in the provided text.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between melanoma and non-melanoma skin lesions. The system classifies lesions into discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma or non-melanoma) using a fusion of handcrafted features and pre-trained CNN features, evaluated via confusion matrices, accuracy, F-measure, and Kappa index on the PH2 and ISIC datasets.

datasets_used.name: PH2 database
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image database for research and benchmarking, collected from the Department of Dermatology, Kaohsiung Medical University.

use.training: Used for training the classification model with fused features and Genetic Algorithm selection.
use.testing: Used for testing the classification performance.

datasets_used.name: ISIC challenge dataset
datasets_used.description: A large-scale dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) challenge, specifically the 2017 challenge data.

use.training: Used for training the classification model with fused features and Genetic Algorithm selection.
use.testing: Used for testing the classification performance.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To divide the dataset into training and test sets to evaluate the model's performance and preserve fairness.
To show the efficiency of the proposed approach and calculate performance metrics such as False Positive rate, Recall, Specificity, Precision, Accuracy, F-measure, and Kappa index.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (Recall, Specificity, Precision, Accuracy, F-measure, Kappa) and comparative analysis of classification results on PH2 and ISIC datasets.

PH2 database.notes: The confusion matrix for the proposed approach with genetic algorithm feature selection on PH2 shows: True Positives (Melanoma) = 37, False Negatives (Melanoma) = 1, False Positives (Non-melanoma) = 3, True Negatives (Non-melanoma) = 159. Comparative studies show accuracies of 93.00% (Salido), 96.5% (Moura), 97.00% (Pathan), 97.5% (Nasir), and 97.5% (Akram).

ISIC Challenge dataset.notes: The confusion matrix for the proposed approach with genetic algorithm feature selection on ISIC shows: True Positives (Melanoma) = 206, False Negatives (Melanoma) = 76, False Positives (Non-melanoma) = 168, True Negatives (Non-melanoma) = 1550. Comparative studies show accuracies of 82.3% (González-Díaz), 82.8% (Matsunaga), 83.83% (Mahbod), and 85.5% (Bi).

architecture_used.main_model: Fusion of handcrafted features and pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet (specifically mentioned for AlexNet which competed in ILSVRC 2012; VggNet, GoogLeNet, and ResNet are also described as pre-trained architectures)

architecture_details.feature_extraction_method: Transfer learning; features extracted from the last fully connected (FC) layer for AlexNet and Vgg16, and from the last convolutional layer for ResNet18 and GoogLeNet
handcrafted_features: Shape features (area, greatest diameter, smallest diameter, perimeter, eccentricity, Exent, Equivalent diameter, Circularity)
Skeleton features (number of Endpoints, Branch-points, Sub-branches, size, length, width, ratio of width/length, max/min length between endpoints)
Color features (White, Red, Light brown, Blue grey, Dark brown, Black percentages)

preprocessing_techniques: Partial Differential Equation (PDE) for artifact removal (hair, blood vessels, skin lines, reflection, ink marking, black frames)
conclusions: The proposed approach, which fuses handcrafted features (shape, skeleton, color, texture) with features from pre-trained CNNs (AlexNet, VggNet, GoogLeNet, ResNet) and uses a Genetic Algorithm for feature selection, effectively combines the strengths of machine learning and deep learning to overcome their respective limitations regarding dataset size.
The fusion of all features (4034 total) provided the best classification results among individual models before feature selection.
The proposed method achieved an accuracy of 98.0%, F-measure of 94.69%, and Kappa index of 96.63% on the PH2 dataset.
The proposed method achieved an accuracy of 87.8%, F-measure of 62.73%, and Kappa index of 55.68% on the ISIC challenge dataset.
The proposed approach outperformed recent comparative studies on both the PH2 and ISIC datasets.
The study acknowledges limitations regarding the limited number of pre-trained and handcrafted methods used.
Future work is identified as extending the use of pre-trained models (e.g., DenseNet, Inception) and increasing training data to improve performance.
A key challenge identified is finding relevant features that perform optimally across different databases.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges a limitation regarding the limited number of pre-trained and handcrafted methods used, suggesting future work should extend to models like DenseNet and Inception.
The paper does not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals; performance is evaluated using descriptive metrics (Accuracy, F-measure, Kappa) and comparative analysis.
The PH2 dataset is small (200 images), which may limit the generalizability of the results compared to the larger ISIC dataset (2000 images).
The ISIC dataset used is from the 2017 challenge, which may be considered outdated compared to more recent ISIC datasets.
The authors state that the manuscript does not contain any studies with human participants performed by any of the authors, relying instead on public databases (PH2 and ISIC).

The proposed approach achieved high accuracy on PH2 (98.0%) but significantly lower performance on ISIC (87.8% accuracy, 62.73% F-measure), indicating potential dataset bias or difficulty in scaling to larger, more diverse datasets.

The feature selection process uses a Genetic Algorithm with specific hyperparameters (population size 20, crossover probability 0.6, mutation probability 0.033), which may introduce variability or overfitting if not robustly validated.
The paper claims to remove artifacts using Partial Differential Equation (PDE) but does not provide detailed quantitative evidence of artifact removal efficacy or segmentation accuracy.
